explicit_text: I am currently a student at the University of Kentucky, about to enter my senior year. I am pursuing a dual degree in environmental and sustainability studies as well as natural resources and environmental science. UK has given me the opportunity to grow academically and socially, especially after the challenges of my first year during the COVID-19 pandemic. I've been able to engage with professors, internships, and the Lexington community, which has been very rewarding.
implicit_text: I am currently a student at a major public university in Lexington, about to enter my senior year. I am pursuing a dual degree in environmental and sustainability studies as well as natural resources and environmental science. This institution has given me the opportunity to grow academically and socially, especially after the challenges of my first year during the COVID-19 pandemic. I've been able to engage with professors, internships, and the local community, which has been very rewarding.

explicit_text: I grew up in Union, Kentucky, which is a suburb in northern Kentucky near Florence. It was a pretty basic and quiet area, but I loved that I could run free in my backyard and explore a small forest and creek nearby. Growing up there gave me a unique experience because I was close to urban areas like Cincinnati but also had access to nature and rural places like Grant County. This mix really shaped my appreciation for both urban and green spaces.

explicit_text: Cincinnati was just about thirty minutes from where I grew up in Union, Kentucky. It was the place we would go to experience a more urban and city-like environment, which contrasted with the natural and rural areas I also visited. Being able to access Cincinnati gave me a broader perspective on different types of environments and communities. It was part of my upbringing that helped me connect urban and green spaces in my mindset.

explicit_text: Grant County, especially Dry Ridge, is where my mom's side of the family lives on a farm. I used to visit them and felt like a little farm girl, living out my dreams of having a garden, horses, and cows. It was a nice rural escape from the suburbs where I lived. Those visits gave me a connection to nature and rural life that balanced my more suburban and urban experiences.

explicit_text: I was somewhat involved with 4-H during my youth. I went to 4-H camp once and occasionally attended events or one-off classes they offered. While I wasn't super involved, I appreciated the opportunities 4-H gave me to connect with nature and learn outside of school. It was one of the few organized activities related to the environment that I participated in before college.

explicit_text: During my high school years from 2016 to 2020, I was aware of Greta Thunberg and her environmental activism. I even had her book, No One Is Too Small to Make a Difference, when it came out and was really excited about it. Her work and the climate change movement influenced me as I started learning more about environmental issues, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic when I had time to reflect on these topics.

cues: No One Is Too Small to Make a Difference

entity: No One Is Too Small to Make a Difference
explicit_text: I read Greta Thunberg's book, No One Is Too Small to Make a Difference, when it was released, and it really inspired me. It came at a time when I was beginning to understand climate change and environmental activism more deeply. The book helped me connect the dots between environmental issues and social justice, especially during the isolation of the COVID-19 pandemic. It was a meaningful part of my journey toward environmental studies.

explicit_text: The COVID-19 pandemic had a huge impact on my last year of high school and my first year at the University of Kentucky. I graduated in 2020 but didn't get to walk at senior year; instead, we had a virtual graduation and a unique ceremony at the Kentucky Speedway. My first year of college was mostly online, which made it hard to meet people and engage fully with my studies. Despite the challenges and the doom and gloom of learning about climate change alone, I managed to get through it and have since grown a lot academically and socially.

My first year of college was mostly online, which made it hard to meet people and engage fully with my studies. Despite the challenges and the doom and gloom of learning about climate change alone, I managed to get through it and have since grown a lot academically and socially.

explicit_text: My high school graduation ceremony was held at the Kentucky Speedway due to the COVID-19 pandemic restrictions. It was quite an unusual experience because we got to do a lap around the track and receive our diplomas there. However, we couldn't mingle or talk with friends, as everything was very controlled for safety. Still, it was a memorable and unique way to graduate that not many people can say they experienced.

explicit_text: Governor Andy Beshear was a familiar figure during the pandemic, especially with his daily briefings. I remember him often saying, 'We'll get through this together,' which was a comforting and hopeful message during such uncertain times. His leadership and words helped many of us cope with the challenges of COVID-19, including the disruptions to school and daily life.
